refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support evaluation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—providing visibility and data—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target is a performance constraint/acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation clarifies that the system must instrument and expose energy data; whether the target is met depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational context.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the system can continuously or periodically measure energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_output (tons moved or equivalent task metric), compute energy_per_ton, and expose this metric via operator interface, logs, or telemetry. Verify accuracy of measurement against calibrated instruments. Confirm data is available for the agreed evaluation period without gaps.
sub_frs: Measure total energy consumed by the NGHE solution during operation.
Measure total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or task-specific metric) during the same period.
Calculate and report energy efficiency metric (energy per unit output) in real-time or near-real-time.
Store and retrieve energy efficiency data for the agreed evaluation period without loss or corruption.
Provide operator and site manager access to energy efficiency metrics via human-machine interface or data export.

candidate_dps: DP1: Continuous dual-channel measurement (energy input + work output) with independent acquisition, buffering, and post-processing pipeline to compute energy_per_ton ratio against target threshold
DP2: Real-time energy and work metering with embedded ratio computation and threshold comparison, triggering alerts when energy_per_ton exceeds E_target
DP3: Periodic sampling of energy consumption and work output at fixed intervals, with batch calculation and reporting of energy_per_ton against target after each evaluation period
DP4: Hybrid approach: continuous low-resolution energy/work sensing with periodic high-resolution calibration snapshots, enabling both real-time trending and accurate final reporting
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Continuous dual-channel measurement (energy input + work output) with independent acquisition, buffering, and post-processing pipeline to compute energy_per_ton ratio against target threshold
selected_dp.rationale_for_selection: DP1 decouples measurement acquisition from computation and reporting, enabling independent validation of each stage. The dual-channel independence (energy ≠ work output measurement) reduces systematic error correlation. Post-processing separation allows offline verification and audit trails required for customer acceptance. Avoids premature threshold logic embedding (DP2) that would couple acceptance criteria into real-time control. Avoids sampling bias risk (DP3) over extended evaluation periods. Simpler than DP4 without sacrificing accuracy.

potential_sources: Energy and work output measurement channels share common power supply or clock → mitigated by independent sensor excitation and timestamping
Post-processing pipeline depends on data buffering capacity → risk if buffer overflow occurs; mitigated by dimensioning buffer for worst-case duty cycle
Acceptance criterion (E_target) embedded in reporting logic → acceptable coupling; target is external input, not internal design assumption
Evaluation period definition couples to data retention policy → acceptable; period is contractual input, not design parameter
coupling_check.notes: No high-risk functional coupling detected. Measurement independence is preserved. Computation pipeline is sequential but not circular. External inputs (E_target, evaluation period) are properly treated as constraints, not design parameters.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy input can be measured at a single point (e.g., main power feed) without loss of fidelity due to distribution losses or auxiliary loads. RISK: If auxiliary systems (lighting, HVAC, safety systems) draw significant power, measured energy will be inflated. MITIGATION: Scope energy measurement to primary work-performing subsystem; document exclusions in acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Work output (tons moved) is directly measurable or derivable from load cell, GPS, or operational logs. RISK: Measurement error in work output directly propagates to energy_per_ton ratio. MITIGATION: Validate work output measurement against independent source (e.g., customer weighbridge records).

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is sufficiently long (days to weeks) to average transient effects and operational variability. RISK: Short periods may not capture representative duty cycles. MITIGATION: Agree on minimum evaluation period length in contract; document operational conditions during measurement window.

ASSUMPTION: Data buffering and post-processing infrastructure is available and reliable (no data loss, no corruption). RISK: Corrupted or missing records invalidate acceptance claim. MITIGATION: Implement checksums, redundant logging, and audit trail for all measurement records.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan does not prohibit continuous measurement infrastructure (e.g., sensor placement, power supply routing). RISK: Safety constraints may force suboptimal sensor locations or measurement gaps. MITIGATION: Coordinate with safety officer early; document any measurement limitations imposed by safety requirements.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined E_target is stable and unambiguous over evaluation period. RISK: Target may be revised mid-evaluation, invalidating comparison. MITIGATION: Freeze E_target in writing before measurement begins; document any changes with timestamp.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Scope of energy measurement (primary work subsystem only, or total system?); (2) Definition of 'ton moved' (gross load, net payload, distance-weighted?); (3) Minimum evaluation period duration and operational conditions (e.g., 'representative 5-day cycle including peak and off-peak hours'); (4) Acceptance criteria precision (e.g., 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target ± 5% measurement uncertainty'); (5) Data retention and audit requirements. These refinements will eliminate ambiguity in DP realization and IM boundary definition.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy measurement and efficiency computation system encompassing dual-channel acquisition (energy input + work output), buffering, post-processing pipeline, and threshold comparison logic

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit computed energy_per_ton ratio, threshold status, and raw measurement streams to monitoring/logging systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive target threshold values, measurement configuration parameters, and pipeline control directives

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit alerts when energy_per_ton ratio exceeds safety-critical thresholds or measurement integrity is compromised

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Synchronize dual-channel measurement timestamps and buffer state across distributed acquisition nodes if multi-site deployment is required

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the natural home for energy-efficiency measurement and optimization logic. Dual-channel acquisition, buffering, and post-processing are core to the NGHE domain. Minor diffusion: threshold comparison may require SafetyAlert interface for critical limits, and Tasking interface for configuration—both manageable via well-defined contracts.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns propulsion, steering, and mechanical subsystems. Energy measurement logic is orthogonal to vehicle control. High diffusion: measurement pipeline would leak into multiple vehicle subsystems (power distribution, motor controllers, sensors), creating tight coupling and maintenance burden.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a command/control interface layer, not a measurement domain. Placing energy measurement here violates separation of concerns. High diffusion: measurement logic would be entangled with remote operator interaction, creating false dependencies and hindering reuse.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns alert generation and safety-critical logic, but not measurement acquisition or post-processing. Placing the full DP here would embed domain-specific energy computation into a cross-cutting safety layer. High diffusion: measurement pipeline would diffuse across Safety Services and other modules, violating cohesion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the authoritative owner of energy measurement and efficiency computation. It encapsulates the dual-channel acquisition strategy, buffering discipline, and post-processing pipeline as a cohesive unit. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide clean contracts for configuration, data export, and alert signaling without leaking internal measurement logic.
containment_risks: Risk: Threshold comparison logic may be interpreted as safety-critical, creating pressure to move it into Safety Services. Mitigation: Clarify that Safety Services consumes the energy_per_ton output and decides on alert severity; NGHE Solution computes the metric only.

Risk: Dual-channel synchronization across distributed nodes may require tight coupling to Vehicle or Teleoperation timing. Mitigation: Use Network Link interface to abstract synchronization; NGHE Solution remains agnostic to physical deployment topology.

Risk: Real-time buffering and post-processing may conflict with Vehicle control loop latency requirements. Mitigation: Define separate acquisition (real-time) and post-processing (batch/deferred) pipelines within NGHE Solution; expose both via Telemetry interface with distinct timestamps.

Risk: Configuration of measurement parameters (e.g., sensor calibration, buffer sizes) may be scattered across Tasking and Vehicle subsystems. Mitigation: Centralize all measurement configuration in NGHE Solution; Tasking interface provides a single control point.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly addresses the FR by specifying a dual-channel measurement strategy (energy input + work output) with independent acquisition, buffering, and post-processing to compute energy_per_ton ratio. The DP is concrete, measurable, and traceable to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). The logic is sound: continuous measurement of both channels, independent buffering to decouple acquisition from processing, and post-processing pipeline to compute the metric against target threshold. No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Threshold comparison logic ownership: Risk that threshold comparison (energy_per_ton vs. E_target) may be interpreted as safety-critical and pressure applied to migrate into Safety Services. Mitigation is clear—NGHE Solution computes the metric; Safety Services consumes it for alert decisions. Recommend explicit interface contract documenting this boundary.

Dual-channel synchronization across distributed nodes: Network Link interface abstracts multi-site synchronization, but tight coupling risk remains if Vehicle or Teleoperation timing constraints force real-time synchronization into their control loops. Mitigation: define synchronization SLA in Network Link contract; NGHE Solution remains topology-agnostic.

Real-time vs. batch processing latency: Buffering and post-processing may conflict with Vehicle control loop latency if both pipelines are conflated. Mitigation is sound—separate real-time acquisition from deferred post-processing; expose both via Telemetry with distinct timestamps. Recommend explicit SLA documentation for each pipeline.

Measurement configuration scatter: Sensor calibration, buffer sizes, and pipeline parameters risk being scattered across Tasking and Vehicle subsystems. Mitigation is strong—centralize all configuration in NGHE Solution via Tasking interface as single control point. Recommend configuration schema document.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary with 0.92 containment score and 0.08 diffusion score. The module encapsulates the complete dual-channel acquisition strategy, buffering discipline, and post-processing pipeline as a cohesive unit. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide clean contracts without leaking internal measurement logic. Vehicle (0.35 containment, 0.65 diffusion) and Teleoperation (0.25 containment, 0.75 diffusion) are correctly rejected as poor fits. Safety Services (0.4 containment, 0.6 diffusion) is correctly scoped to alert generation only, not measurement acquisition. IM boundary is well-defined and justified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over evaluation period) is directly computable from NGHE Solution outputs. Telemetry interface exports energy_per_ton ratio and threshold status, enabling independent verification against customer-defined targets. Raw measurement streams are also exported for audit and post-hoc analysis. Worksite safety plan integration is addressed via SafetyAlert interface for critical threshold exceedances. Measurement integrity monitoring is explicitly mentioned in SafetyAlert scope, supporting verification of data quality.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated entirely to NGHE Solution IM boundary. Dual-channel acquisition (energy input + work output) is owned by NGHE Solution. Buffering and post-processing pipeline are owned by NGHE Solution. Threshold comparison logic is owned by NGHE Solution (with Safety Services consuming the output for alert decisions). Configuration and control are centralized via Tasking interface. Data export is centralized via Telemetry interface. No ambiguity in allocation across modules. Interfaces are well-defined with clear direction (in/out/inout) and contract scope.

recommended_revisions: Document explicit interface contract for Tasking: specify measurement configuration schema (sensor calibration parameters, buffer sizes, pipeline control directives, target threshold E_target). Include SLA for configuration propagation latency.

Document explicit interface contract for Telemetry: specify output schema for energy_per_ton ratio, threshold status, raw measurement streams, and timestamps. Define separate SLAs for real-time acquisition telemetry vs. batch post-processing telemetry.

Document explicit interface contract for SafetyAlert: clarify that NGHE Solution emits alerts only when measurement integrity is compromised or energy_per_ton exceeds pre-defined safety-critical thresholds (distinct from customer-defined E_target). Safety Services decides on alert severity and operator notification.

Document explicit interface contract for Network Link: specify synchronization protocol for dual-channel timestamps across distributed acquisition nodes. Define synchronization tolerance (e.g., ±10ms) and fallback behavior if synchronization fails.

Add acceptance test plan: specify how energy_per_ton metric will be computed, validated, and reported over the evaluation period. Include audit trail for raw measurement streams to support post-hoc verification against customer targets.

Clarify real-time vs. batch processing SLAs: document latency requirements for real-time acquisition (e.g., <100ms) vs. post-processing pipeline (e.g., batch every 5 minutes). Ensure Vehicle control loop does not depend on post-processing latency.

Add configuration management plan: specify how measurement parameters (calibration, buffer sizes, target threshold) are versioned, deployed, and audited. Ensure single source of truth in NGHE Solution.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is concrete and measurable; the IM boundary (NGHE Solution) is well-justified with 0.92 containment score and clear interface contracts. However, the high safety criticality of the energy_per_ton metric and the complexity of dual-channel synchronization, real-time vs. batch processing latency, and measurement configuration scatter warrant decomposition into sub-FRs to ensure each critical concern is explicitly addressed at the next level. The recommended revisions (interface contracts, SLAs, configuration management, acceptance test plan) are substantial enough to benefit from structured sub-FR derivation rather than ad-hoc documentation. Decomposition will also clarify ownership of synchronization logic, latency guarantees, and configuration management as distinct functional concerns.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Acquire energy input measurements continuously from distributed sources with synchronized timestamps and configurable calibration parameters.
FR1.2: Acquire work output measurements continuously from distributed sources with synchronized timestamps and configurable calibration parameters.
FR1.3: Buffer acquired measurements independently to decouple real-time acquisition from post-processing without loss or reordering.
FR1.4: Compute energy_per_ton ratio from buffered measurements using post-processing pipeline with configurable aggregation window and target threshold.
FR1.5: Export energy_per_ton metric, threshold status, and raw measurement streams via Telemetry interface with distinct SLAs for real-time and batch pipelines.
FR1.6: Emit safety-critical alerts when measurement integrity is compromised or energy_per_ton exceeds pre-defined safety thresholds, with audit trail.
FR1.7: Centralize measurement configuration (calibration, buffer sizes, pipeline parameters, target threshold) via Tasking interface with version control and deployment audit trail.
FR1.8: Synchronize dual-channel timestamps across distributed acquisition nodes within defined tolerance to ensure measurement coherence.

revise.why: No revisions needed at current level. FR, DP, and IM are sound. Decomposition is the appropriate next step to address safety criticality and architectural complexity.

assumptions: Energy input and work output can be measured independently with sufficient fidelity to compute energy_per_ton ratio within customer-defined accuracy bounds.
Dual-channel synchronization tolerance (e.g., ±10ms) is achievable across distributed acquisition nodes without real-time control loop intervention.
Post-processing latency (e.g., batch every 5 minutes) is acceptable for energy_per_ton reporting and does not conflict with Vehicle control loop requirements.
Safety Services will consume energy_per_ton metric and threshold status from NGHE Solution without re-implementing measurement logic.
Tasking interface can reliably propagate measurement configuration (calibration, buffer sizes, target threshold) to NGHE Solution with acceptable latency.
Network Link abstraction is sufficient to handle multi-site synchronization without exposing timing constraints to Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems.
open_risks: Threshold comparison logic ownership: Risk that Safety Services or Vehicle subsystems may attempt to re-implement or override energy_per_ton threshold logic. Mitigation: explicit interface contract and ownership documentation required at sub-FR level.

Dual-channel synchronization failure modes: If synchronization tolerance is violated, measurement coherence is compromised. Sub-FR1.8 must define fallback behavior (e.g., alert, skip batch, use last-known offset).

Real-time vs. batch processing latency conflict: If Vehicle control loop inadvertently depends on post-processing pipeline latency, system may exhibit unpredictable behavior. Sub-FR1.5 must define separate SLAs and ensure Vehicle does not consume batch telemetry.

Measurement configuration scatter: If calibration parameters or buffer sizes are not centralized in NGHE Solution, configuration drift and audit trail loss may occur. Sub-FR1.7 must enforce single source of truth.

Safety-critical alert generation: If measurement integrity alerts are conflated with customer-defined threshold alerts, Safety Services may over-alert or under-alert. Sub-FR1.6 must distinguish alert types and define severity mapping.

Acceptance test plan feasibility: If energy_per_ton metric cannot be independently verified against customer targets, acceptance may be disputed. Sub-FR1.5 must ensure raw measurement streams are exported with sufficient fidelity for post-hoc audit.
evidence_needed: Synchronization protocol specification for Network Link interface: define timestamp format, synchronization algorithm, tolerance bounds (e.g., ±10ms), and fallback behavior.
Measurement configuration schema: specify calibration parameter ranges, buffer size constraints, pipeline control directives, and target threshold format.
Telemetry output schema: specify energy_per_ton ratio format, threshold status encoding, raw measurement stream format, and timestamp precision.
SafetyAlert interface contract: distinguish measurement integrity alerts from threshold exceedance alerts; define severity levels and operator notification rules.

Real-time vs. batch processing SLA document: specify latency requirements for real-time acquisition (<100ms assumed), batch post-processing (5-minute window assumed), and Vehicle control loop independence.
Configuration management plan: specify version control, deployment audit trail, and rollback procedures for measurement parameters.
Acceptance test plan: specify how energy_per_ton metric will be computed, validated, and reported over evaluation period; include audit trail for raw measurement streams.
Measurement fidelity analysis: confirm that energy input and work output measurements can be acquired with sufficient accuracy to meet customer-defined energy_per_ton tolerance bounds.
